data.label: Errors found?
data.description: Gate the email on whether the Loki query actually returned error lines. The query already filters for error/fatal/panic/exception, so `query_logs.entry_count > 0` means real errors were found. >0 → send the alert; otherwise skip the email and finish quietly via a separate End (send_alert never runs on that path, so its outputs aren't available to map).

data.label: Send Alert Email
data.description: SMTP backend with Tera templates. Subject + plain-text + HTML bodies are rendered against the upstream Loki count and the agent's summary (`{{ summarize.response }}`). The transport (host/port/auth/from) comes from the workspace resource `mail`, bound at launch time. Recipient is hard-coded to the on-call address; edit `to` or promote it to a Start field to change it. Dry-run is off.

body_html.source:
Aithericon cluster error-log alert

Scan time: {{ start.fire_time }}
Window: last 15 minutes
Matched lines: {{ query_logs.entry_count }}

{{ summarize.response }}

— Aithericon monitoring (Loki Error-Log Alert)
body_html.label: body.html.tera

body_text.label: body.txt.tera

subject.source: [ALERT] {{ query_logs.entry_count }} error log line(s) in the Aithericon cluster
subject.label: subject.tera

data.label: Query Error Logs
data.description: Loki `query_range` backend. Runs the LogQL query `{nomad_task=~".+", nomad_task!="loki", nomad_task!="grafana", nomad_task!="tempo"} | logfmt | level=~"(?i)error|fatal|critical|panic"` against the `aithericon_loki` resource over a 15m lookback (`since: 15m`, end defaults to now), `limit: 8`, `direction: backward`. It parses `logfmt` and keeps only genuine error-level lines via the `level` field (`error|fatal|critical|panic`, case-insensitive) — not a substring grep for the word 'error', which would pull in INFO lines that merely mention it. The observability stack itself (`nomad_task!="loki"`, `!="grafana"`, `!="tempo"`) is excluded so the alert isn't tripped by Loki's own query-retry logs or Grafana's auth-parse noise — those are an artifact of *querying* Loki, not real incidents. `limit` is kept small (8) on purpose: the full entry envelope (line + labels) feeds the downstream Agent, and too many entries overflow a typical 32K-token model context. The query is static (no `{{ }}` placeholders). Output is the fixed Loki envelope: `entries`, `entry_count`, `series`, `result_type`, `stats`.

data.label: Summarize Errors
data.description: Single-shot Agent (maxTurns 1, no tools — the degenerate LLM path). Reads the matched error log entries from the upstream Loki step (`{{ query_logs.entries }}` / `{{ query_logs.entry_count }}`, resolved via a synthesized read-arc) and writes a concise incident summary: how many lines matched, which tasks/services are affected, the most likely root cause, and a one-line recommended action. The summary is parked in the agent's `response` output for the SMTP step to template against. Bound to Hugging Face Inference via `provider: openai` + `resourceAlias: hf_inference`; billed to the `Aithericon` HF org via the `X-HF-Bill-To` header (emitted from the resource's `organization` field). `model` is `Qwen/Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct`.
data.systemPrompt: You are an SRE on-call assistant. You receive raw error-level log lines pulled from a Grafana Loki instance and turn them into a short, scannable incident summary for an alert email. Be concise and factual — no preamble. Structure your reply as: (1) one headline sentence with the total count and the time window; (2) a short bulleted list grouping the errors by affected task/service with the dominant error pattern for each; (3) the single most likely root cause; (4) one recommended next action. If there are no entries, say clearly that no error-level logs were found in the window.

data.label: Every 12 hours
data.description: Cron trigger. Fires the workflow on a fixed schedule (default `0 0 */12 * * *` — every 12 hours, at 00:00 and 12:00 UTC). NOTE: the engine's cron parser expects a 6-field expression (leading seconds field), not the 5-field crontab form. The cron source exposes `fire_time` and `scheduled_time`; `payloadMapping` forwards `fire_time` onto the Start `fire_time` field so the alert email can report when the check ran.
